               The Secretary of Labor has determined that
               the publication of this periodical is necessary
               in the transaction of the public business re-
               quired by law of this Department. Use of funds
               for printing this periodical has been approved
               by the Director of the Office of Management
               and Budget through February 28, 1981. Con-
               trolled circulation postage paid at Riverdale,
               Md.

               Comparison of Nonagricultural
               Employment Estimates From
               Two Surveys
               "Gloria Peterson Green




                 Each month the Bureau of Labor Statistics analyzes         does not cover employees unless they are paid—on the
               and publishes two independently derived estimates of         payroll—during the reference period.
               nonagricultural employment, based on data collected             The household survey provides information on the
               from the Current Population Survey (household survey)        labor force activity of the entire civilian noninstitutional
               and the Current Employment Survey (establishment             population 16 years of age and over, without duplica-
               survey). Both series are important in appraising current     tion, since each individual is classified as either
               labor market trends and in assessing the overall perfor-     employed, unemployed, or not in the labor force. Per-
               mance of the economy. Data from the household survey         sons holding more than one job are classified according
               are obtained from a sample of 56,000 households1 and         to the job at which they worked the most hours. The
               measure the work status of individuals, whereas data         establishment survey has no age qualification, and
               from the establishment survey are derived from payroll       employees working at more than one job or otherwise
               records of approximately 162,000 firms employing over        appearing on more than one payroll are counted
               30 million workers and are essentially a count of oc-        separately for each appearance.
               cupied jobs.

               as the 1977-79 period, their movements have been             for 1977-79, with identification of quantifiable dif-
               generally comparable. Differences do occur, at times,        ferences. The reconciliation technique involves a series
               with respect to the magnitude and direction of month-        of adjustments to the total nonagricultural employment
               to-month changes and in the timing and extent of             estimate of the household survey to bring it into as close
               business cycle swings. At least some of these differences    conformity as possible with the definition of employ-
               arise because the series measure somewhat different          ment in the establishment survey. Thus, estimates for
               phenomena—employed individuals versus paid jobs.             the following groups are subtracted from the household

                      Spendable earnings are calculated by taking the average weekly pay for all production
               or nonsupervisory jobs, both full-time and part-time, and then deducting social security and

               dents who earned this amount (see Explanatory Notes for the establishment data in the back of

              Labor turnover
                                                                                           The "link relative" technique
                 Labor turnover is the gross movement of wage and salary workers
              into and out of employed status with respect to individual                      From a sample composed of establishments reporting for both the
              establishments. This movement, which relates to a calendar month, is         previous and current months, the ratio of current month employment
              divided into two broad types: Accessions (new hires and rehires) and         to that of the previous month is computed. This is called a "link
              separations (terminations of employment initiated by either employer         relative." The estimates of employment (all employees, including pro-
              or employee). Each type of action is cumulated for a calendar month          duction and nonproduction workers together) for the current month
              and expressed as a rate per 100 employees. The data relate to all            are obtained by multiplying the estimates for the previous month by
              employees, whether full- or part-time, permanent or temporary, in-           these "link relatives." In addition, small bias correction factors are
              cluding executive, office, sales, other salaried personnel, and produc-      applied to selected employment estimates each month. The size of the
              tion workers.                                                                bias correction factors is determined from past experience. Other
                                                                                           features of the general procedures are described in table J.
                 Accessions are the total number of permanent and temporary addi-
              tions to the employment roll, including both new and rehired
              employees.                                                                   Size and regional stratification
                 New hires are temporary or permanent additions to the employment

              ment (except employees transferring from another establishment of            and/or by region, and the stratified production- or nonsupervisory

                  Recalls are permanent or temporary additions to the employment           a size stratum, a region stratum, or a size stratum of a region within an
               roll of persons specfically recalled to a job in the same establishment     industry. The labor turnover estimates are stratified by industry only.
               of the company following a period of layoff lasting more than 7 con-
               secutive days. (The collection of recalls, as a separate uem, began         Benchmark adjustments

               measure is the standard deviation adjusted for the bias in estimates:                                                   Root- mean- square

                     RMSE           (Standard Deviation) + (Bias)

               If the bias is small, the chances are about 2 out of 3 that an estimate                                                Monthly

               from the sample would differ from its benchmark by less than the                                                        level

               root-mean-square error. The chances are about 19 out of 20 that the
               difference would be less than twice the root-mean-square error.
